event: The Yenisei Kyrgyz are first mentioned in Chinese sources, marking the earliest historical record of the Kyrgyz people in the Yenisei River region of southern Siberia.
The Xiongnu confederation under Modu Chanyu extends its control over the Kyrgyz and other peoples of the Central Asian steppe.
The Turkic Khaganate emerges as the dominant steppe power, bringing the Kyrgyz lands into the orbit of the early Turkic empires.
The Yenisei Kyrgyz overthrow the Uyghur Khaganate and establish the Kyrgyz Khaganate, dominating much of Central Asia and Mongolia.
The Kyrgyz Khaganate loses control of Mongolia to the rising Khitan Liao dynasty, contracting Kyrgyz power back toward the Yenisei region.
The Turkic Karakhanid dynasty consolidates control over the region of modern Kyrgyzstan, promoting the spread of Islam among the local population.
Yusuf Balasaguni completes the Kutadgu Bilig in Balasagun, a landmark work of Turkic literature produced under Karakhanid patronage.
The Qara Khitai (Western Liao) defeat the Seljuks at the Battle of Qatwan and incorporate the region, including Balasagun, into their realm.
The Yenisei Kyrgyz submit to Genghis Khan, becoming among the first peoples to be incorporated into the rising Mongol Empire.
Genghis Khan's Mongol armies conquer Central Asia, and the lands of modern Kyrgyzstan become part of the Chagatai Khanate.
Kyrgyz tribes, having migrated south into the Tian Shan mountains over preceding centuries, consolidate as a distinct people in the region they inhabit today.
The Dzungar (Oirat) Khanate invades and subjugates much of Kyrgyz territory, pushing many Kyrgyz tribes into the mountains.
The Qing dynasty destroys the Dzungar Khanate, and many Kyrgyz tribes acknowledge nominal Qing suzerainty.
The Khanate of Kokand extends its rule over southern Kyrgyz lands, building fortresses such as Bishkek (Pishpek) to control the region.
The Bugu tribe of the northern Kyrgyz accepts Russian protection, beginning the incorporation of Kyrgyz lands into the Russian Empire.
Russian forces, allied with local Kyrgyz, capture and destroy the Kokand fortress of Pishpek, the predecessor of modern Bishkek.
Russia abolishes the Khanate of Kokand and annexes the southern Kyrgyz lands, completing the conquest of present-day Kyrgyzstan.
The Central Asian Revolt erupts against Russian conscription, and its brutal suppression drives many Kyrgyz to flee to China in the catastrophe known as the Urkun.
Soviet rule is established over the Kyrgyz region as it becomes part of the Turkestan Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic within the Russian SFSR.
The Soviet national delimitation of Central Asia creates the Kara-Kirghiz Autonomous Oblast, the first distinct administrative homeland for the Kyrgyz.
The territory is upgraded to the Kirghiz Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic within the Russian SFSR.
The Kirghiz Soviet Socialist Republic is established as a full union republic of the Soviet Union under the new Soviet constitution.
Stalin's Great Purge devastates the Kyrgyz political and intellectual elite, with thousands executed or imprisoned.
Writer Chinghiz Aitmatov publishes Jamila, beginning a literary career that would make him Kyrgyzstan's most celebrated cultural figure.
Ethnic clashes erupt between Kyrgyz and Uzbeks in the Osh region, and Askar Akayev is elected president of the Kirghiz SSR.
Kyrgyzstan declares independence from the Soviet Union on 31 August and Askar Akayev wins the first direct presidential election in October.
Kyrgyzstan is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign state on 2 March.
Kyrgyzstan adopts its first post-Soviet constitution and introduces its national currency, the som, replacing the Russian ruble.
Askar Akayev is re-elected president as Kyrgyzstan pursues market reforms and is praised as an 'island of democracy' in Central Asia.
Kyrgyzstan becomes the first former Soviet state to join the World Trade Organization.
Islamic Movement of Uzbekistan militants stage armed incursions into southern Kyrgyzstan, taking hostages in the Batken conflict.
Kyrgyzstan grants the United States use of the Manas Air Base to support military operations in nearby Afghanistan.
The Tulip Revolution ousts President Askar Akayev amid protests over fraudulent elections, bringing Kurmanbek Bakiyev to power.
A violent uprising overthrows President Bakiyev in April, followed by deadly ethnic clashes between Kyrgyz and Uzbeks in the south in June.
Voters approve a new constitution establishing a parliamentary system, making Kyrgyzstan the first parliamentary democracy in Central Asia.
Almazbek Atambayev is elected president in a peaceful transfer of power, and Roza Otunbayeva completes her term as interim president.
The United States closes the Manas Transit Center, ending its military presence in Kyrgyzstan.
Kyrgyzstan formally joins the Russian-led Eurasian Economic Union, deepening economic integration with Russia and Kazakhstan.
Sooronbay Jeenbekov is elected president, marking the first peaceful democratic transition between elected leaders in the country's history.
Disputed parliamentary elections trigger mass protests that topple the government and propel Sadyr Japarov to power.
Sadyr Japarov wins the presidency and a referendum approves a new constitution restoring a strong presidential system.
Deadly border clashes erupt between Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan, the worst fighting in a long-running dispute over the Fergana Valley frontier.
